stem: The nurse is caring for a 78-year-old male client with limited mobility who has developed a new area of skin breakdown. After reviewing the client's electronic health record, complete the bowtie by selecting the appropriate actions, the potential condition, and the parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: Clinical Feed (SBAR)
tabs.content:
Situation: This is the 15:00 SBAR for Mr. Johnson, a 78-year-old male in room 402, who has developed a new Stage I pressure ulcer on his coccyx.
Background: Mr. Johnson has a history of stroke with left-sided weakness and limited mobility. At 09:00, a 2 cm x 2 cm area of non-blanchable erythema with intact skin was noted on his coccyx. A hydrocolloid dressing was applied per protocol.
Assessment: Vitals are stable and he denies pain at the site. Labs are unremarkable, with prealbumin at 16 mg/dL. Mr. Johnson is alert and oriented.
Recommendation: Continue repositioning every 2 hours. Monitor the site for any changes in size, color, or drainage. Encourage adequate nutrition and hydration. Consider a pressure-redistributing mattress overlay.

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content:
General: Alert and oriented x3. Appears stated age.
Skin: 2 cm x 2 cm area of non-blanchable erythema on coccyx with intact skin. No drainage or odor noted. Surrounding skin is warm and dry.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, S1 and S2 auscultated. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops noted. Peripheral pulses +2 bilaterally.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No cough or adventitious breath sounds.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, normoactive bowel sounds in all four quadrants. No distension.
Neurological: Gross motor strength 4/5 in left upper and lower extremities due to prior stroke. Sensory intact to light touch bilaterally. Alert and oriented to person, place, and time.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content:
Nursing Priorities:
  1. Protect skin integrity and prevent further breakdown.
  2. Promote wound healing.
  3. Manage risk factors contributing to pressure ulcer development.
Nursing Interventions:
  • Assess pressure ulcer daily for changes in size, color, and drainage using PUSH tool.
  • Apply hydrocolloid dressing to coccyx pressure ulcer per wound care protocol. Change dressing every 3 days or PRN.
  • Reposition patient every 2 hours using a written schedule. Ensure proper body alignment and support with pillows.
  • Encourage adequate hydration (2000 mL/day) and nutrition. Consult dietitian for nutritional assessment and recommendations.
  • Implement pressure redistribution strategies: Use a pressure-reducing mattress overlay.
  • Consult with wound care specialist for further evaluation and recommendations.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: X-Ray, Lumbar Spine (3 days prior): Mild degenerative changes noted. No acute fractures or dislocations. Soft tissues appear unremarkable.

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content:
MedicationDosageRouteFrequencyScheduled Time
Multivitamin1 tabletPODaily09:00
Acetaminophen650 mgPOq4h PRN painAs needed
Docusate Sodium100 mgPOBID09:00, 21:00

physicalExam.skin: 2 cm x 2 cm area of non-blanchable erythema on coccyx with intact skin. No drainage or odor noted. Surrounding skin is warm and dry.
physicalExam.neurological: Gross motor strength 4/5 in left upper and lower extremities due to prior stroke.

nursingOrders: Assess pressure ulcer daily for changes in size, color, and drainage using PUSH tool.
Apply hydrocolloid dressing to coccyx pressure ulcer per wound care protocol. Change dressing every 3 days or PRN.
Reposition patient every 2 hours using a written schedule. Ensure proper body alignment and support with pillows.
Encourage adequate hydration (2000 mL/day) and nutrition. Consult dietitian for nutritional assessment and recommendations.
Implement pressure redistribution strategies: Use a pressure-reducing mattress overlay.
Consult with wound care specialist for further evaluation and recommendations.

actions.text: Elevate the head of the bed to 45 degrees

hoverRationales.rationale: Correct. Relieving pressure on the coccyx restores blood flow and oxygenation to the ischemic tissue. This is the primary intervention to prevent the progression of a Stage I pressure ulcer and promote healing.
Correct. A hydrocolloid dressing protects the intact epidermis from friction and shear. It creates a moist, occlusive environment that supports healing of the underlying compromised tissue.

Incorrect. Debridement is the removal of necrotic tissue. This client's skin is intact with non-blanchable erythema, indicating no necrosis is present. Performing debridement would cause a break in the skin and increase infection risk.

Incorrect. Elevating the head of the bed to 45 degrees increases shearing forces and direct pressure on the coccyx and sacrum. This action would counteract pressure redistribution efforts and likely worsen the tissue injury.

Incorrect. A deep tissue injury presents as a purple or maroon area of discoloration or a blood-filled blister. This client's assessment describes non-blanchable erythema (redness), which is inconsistent with a DTI.

Correct. The client's physical exam reveals a localized area of intact skin with non-blanchable erythema over a bony prominence (coccyx). This is the classic clinical presentation of a Stage I pressure ulcer.

Incorrect. A Stage II pressure ulcer involves partial-thickness skin loss, presenting as a blister or shallow crater with an exposed dermis. This client's skin is documented as being intact, which rules out this diagnosis.

Incorrect. An unstageable ulcer is a full-thickness wound where the base is obscured by slough or eschar. This client's skin is intact, and the wound base is not applicable, making this diagnosis clinically incorrect.

Correct. Tracking the wound's dimensions, color, and skin integrity provides objective data to evaluate the effectiveness of interventions. Worsening characteristics, such as increased size or skin breakdown, indicate progression of tissue ischemia.

Correct. Pain is a subjective indicator of tissue injury and inflammation. An increase in pain can signal worsening tissue damage or the onset of a complication, such as infection, even before visible changes occur.

Incorrect. While important for long-term healing, serum albumin has a long half-life and reflects nutritional status over weeks, not daily changes. Monitoring this is not a priority for tracking the acute status of a Stage I ulcer.

Incorrect. While adequate hydration is important for tissue perfusion, urine output is a general indicator of fluid status and renal function. It is not a specific or direct parameter for monitoring a localized Stage I pressure ulcer.

topicTags: Pressure Ulcer Staging and Wound Care

pearls.description: Early identification and intervention are crucial in preventing the progression of pressure ulcers. Stage I ulcers are reversible with appropriate care.
pearls.strategy: Regular skin assessments, pressure redistribution, and proper wound care can prevent further tissue damage.

pearls.description: Hydrocolloid dressings promote a moist wound environment, which facilitates healing and protects the skin from friction and shear.
pearls.strategy: Ensure the dressing is properly applied and changed according to protocol to maintain optimal wound healing conditions.

traps.description: Elevating the head of the bed can increase pressure and shear forces on the sacrum and coccyx, potentially worsening the pressure ulcer.
traps.strategy: Keep the head of the bed at the lowest possible elevation (≤30 degrees) unless contraindicated. Use pillows to support the patient and prevent sliding.

traps.description: Debridement of a Stage I pressure ulcer is contraindicated as the skin is intact. Debridement in this case can cause skin breakdown and increase the risk of infection.
traps.strategy: Only debride wounds with necrotic tissue. For Stage I ulcers, focus on pressure relief and protection of the intact skin.

mnemonics.description: Pressure Ulcer Scale for Healing (wound assessment tool)

answerBreakdown.a1: Relieving pressure on the coccyx restores blood flow and oxygenation to the ischemic tissue. This is the primary intervention to prevent the progression of a Stage I pressure ulcer and promote healing.
answerBreakdown.a2: A hydrocolloid dressing protects the intact epidermis from friction and shear. It creates a moist, occlusive environment that supports healing of the underlying compromised tissue.
answerBreakdown.a3: Debridement is the removal of necrotic tissue. This client's skin is intact with non-blanchable erythema, indicating no necrosis is present. Performing debridement would cause a break in the skin and increase infection risk.
answerBreakdown.a4: Elevating the head of the bed to 45 degrees increases shearing forces and direct pressure on the coccyx and sacrum. This action would counteract pressure redistribution efforts and likely worsen the tissue injury.
answerBreakdown.p1: Tracking the wound's dimensions, color, and skin integrity provides objective data to evaluate the effectiveness of interventions. Worsening characteristics, such as increased size or skin breakdown, indicate progression of tissue ischemia.
answerBreakdown.p2: Pain is a subjective indicator of tissue injury and inflammation. An increase in pain can signal worsening tissue damage or the onset of a complication, such as infection, even before visible changes occur.
answerBreakdown.p3: While important for long-term healing, serum albumin has a long half-life and reflects nutritional status over weeks, not daily changes. Monitoring this is not a priority for tracking the acute status of a Stage I ulcer.
answerBreakdown.p4: While adequate hydration is important for tissue perfusion, urine output is a general indicator of fluid status and renal function. It is not a specific or direct parameter for monitoring a localized Stage I pressure ulcer.
answerBreakdown.Deep Tissue Injury: A deep tissue injury presents as a purple or maroon area of discoloration or a blood-filled blister. This client's assessment describes non-blanchable erythema (redness), which is inconsistent with a DTI.
answerBreakdown.Stage I Pressure Ulcer: The client's physical exam reveals a localized area of intact skin with non-blanchable erythema over a bony prominence (coccyx). This is the classic clinical presentation of a Stage I pressure ulcer.
answerBreakdown.Stage II Pressure Ulcer: A Stage II pressure ulcer involves partial-thickness skin loss, presenting as a blister or shallow crater with an exposed dermis. This client's skin is documented as being intact, which rules out this diagnosis.
answerBreakdown.Unstageable Pressure Ulcer: An unstageable ulcer is a full-thickness wound where the base is obscured by slough or eschar. This client's skin is intact, and the wound base is not applicable, making this diagnosis clinically incorrect.
